nodes.title: Autonomy II
nodes.description: Covers principles and methods for perception and localization of autonomous robotic systems within a systematic software framework. The analysis and design of practical methods for the deployment of control, path planning, localization and mapping, and safety of autonomous systems are provided in the context of the Robotic Operative Systems (ROS). Topics covered: introduction to ROS; perception using RGB-D cameras and lidar; Kalman filters for state estimation; SLAM; and path planning of robotic manipulators. Prerequisite: Knowledge of control systems (EE 3515 or similar) is recommended.
nodes.other_requirements: Knowledge of control systems ( EE 3515 or similar) is recommended. Students are expected to attend all lectures on time. Student interactions with the instructor are vital for succeeding in the course. You are expected to spend considerable time outside of class working on the material covered during lectures. There will be homework with software-based problems that are expected to be significantly attempted by students; otherwise, the homework will receive a grade of zero.

nodes.title: Semiconductor Materials/Devic
nodes.description: Covers Energy band theory, effective mass, band structure and electronic properties of semiconductors. Transport of electrons and holes in bulk materials and across interfaces. MOSFETs, BJTs, pn junctions, and Schottky barriers. Experimental portion of course will cover electronic measurements of semiconductor devices. Credit not awarded for both PHYS 3675 and PHYS 5675. Prerequisites: EE 2145, EE 3410, or PHYS 3300. Cross-listed with: EE 3440.
nodes.other_requirements: Must enroll in PHYS 3675 A01 or A02; In person attendance is required. If you miss more than 3 unexcused classes during the semester you will receive an F in the class.

nodes.title: Convex Optimization
nodes.description: Provides advanced mathematical tools to recognize optimization problems from applications, presents rigorous theory of convex optimization with an emphasis on results that are helpful for implementation/computation/modeling, providing student with the experience and understanding necessary to use the tools in their own research work or applications. Prerequisites: Linear Algebra, multi-variable calculus.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission, class standing, GPA.

nodes.title: Calculus III
nodes.description: Vector-valued functions. Calculus of functions of several variables: partial derivatives, gradient, divergence, curl, multiple integrals, line integrals, Stokes' and Green's theorems. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1248 or MATH 1242.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1248 or MATH 1242

nodes.title: Communication Theory/Practice
nodes.description: Fundamentals of digital communications including PCM, source and channel coding, pulse shaping and modulation; wireless communications, modulation, antennas and link budgets; application of probability; related laboratory experience. Prerequisite: Graduate standing or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate standing or Instructor permission.

nodes.title: Engineering Ethics/Leadership
nodes.description: Rights and responsibilities in engineering practice and research. Case studies related to engineering ethics. Ethics and professional practice as related to professional licensure. Development of individual leadership abilities. Team-based development of written reports and oral presentations. Prerequisite: Minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Junior standing

nodes.title: EE Principles and Design
nodes.description: Hands-on introduction to contemporary electrical engineering principles and practice. Basic analog and digital circuit design, construction, operation, measurement. Interfacing sensors and actuators to a microcontroller, programming to interact with the world. Individual and team-based assignments that develop data dexterity and technical communication skills. Exposure to breadth of discipline and ethics in the profession. Design project. Prerequisite: First-Year students only.
nodes.other_requirements: First-Year students only. Must register for lab EE 1100 A01

nodes.title: Electric Energy Systems
nodes.description: Electrical safety; Electric power (DC, AC, single and multiphase) and transmission lines; Electric transformers; DC and AC generators; DC and AC motors; Related applications (examples: pumped hydro, HVDC transmission lines, drives); Laboratory included. Prerequisite: C- or better in EE 2135, EE 2145, EE 2155, or EE 2175.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in EE 2135, or B- or better in EE 2145 or B- or better in EE 2175. Must register for a lab section A01 - A02

nodes.title: Circuits I
nodes.description: Fundamental DC circuit analysis course with lab component. Topics: circuit elements and variables, integrated circuits, basic laws of circuits, method of circuit analysis. Elements of design and sensors are introduced. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1248 or MATH 1242.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in the prerequisite courses. Must register for a lab section A01 - A02.

nodes.title: Capstone Design II
nodes.description: Project-based course. Multidisciplinary teams apply their knowledge to design, analyze, build and test a functional prototype that meets client's requirements and solves their problems. Teams follow engineering design and project management processes such as periodic reports, presentations, meetings, reviews and demonstrations using standard industry tools. Prerequisite: EE 4100. Cross-listed with: ME 4020.
nodes.other_requirements: Senior standing required

nodes.title: Statistics for Engineering
nodes.description: Data analysis, probability models, parameter estimation, hypothesis testing. Multi- factor experimental design and regression analysis. Quality control, SPC, reliability. Engineering cases and project. Statistical analysis software. Credit not awarded for both STAT 1410 and STAT 2430. Prerequisites: MATH 1212 or MATH 1234.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum sophomore standing; Open to Degree and PACE students

nodes.title: College Honors
nodes.description: Honors studies leading to thesis. Prerequisite: CEMS 2010.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Calculus II
nodes.description: Vectors and vector operations. Techniques and applications of integration. Polar coordinates, Taylor polynomials, sequences and series, power series. Credit will not be given for both MATH 1248 and MATH 1242. No credit following receipt of credit for MATH 2248 or above. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1234.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1234. C- or better in MATH 021.

nodes.title: Gr Semiconductor Materials/Dev
nodes.description: Covers energy band theory, effective mass, band structure and electronic properties of semiconductors. Transport of electrons and holes in bulk materials and across interfaces. MOSFETs, BJTs, pn junctions, and Schottky barriers. Experimental portion of course will have a laboratory component for electronic measurements of semiconductor devices. Credit not awarded for both EE 5440 and EE 3440. Prerequisite: Graduate student or Instructor permission. Cross-listed with: PHYS 5675.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student or Instructor permission; Must register for EE 5440 A01 or A02 lab; In person attendance is required. If you miss more than 3 unexcused classes during the semester you will receive an F in the class.

nodes.title: Electrical Circuits & Sensors
nodes.description: Fundamentals of electrical circuits with applications to the use of sensors. DC and AC circuits. Sensors utilized for civil engineering and environmental engineering applications. Demonstrations, hands-on exercises. No credit for more than one of EE 003, EE 2125, EE 2175, EE 2145. Prerequisites: MATH 1248 or MATH 1242; CS 1210.
nodes.other_requirements: No credit for more than one of EE 003, EE 2125, EE 2175, EE 2145. Must sign up for EE 2175 A01-A03. For CEE, EENV, ENGR, EMGT; Open to Degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Capstone Design I
nodes.description: Project-based course. Multidisciplinary teams apply their knowledge to design, analyze, build and test a functional prototype that meets client's requirements and solves unique problems. Teams follow engineering design and project management processes such as periodic reports, presentations, meetings, reviews and demonstrations using standard industry tools. Prerequisite: Senior standing in Mechanical or Biomedical Engineering or Instructor permission. Cross-listed with: ME 4010.
nodes.other_requirements: Senior standing in Mechanical or Electrical Engineering or Instructor permission

nodes.title: Circuits Design Project
nodes.description: Project-based course focused on the design of circuits for analog-to-digital and digital-to-analog conversion, analog computing with operational amplifiers, and filtering of signals. Advanced instrumentation, fabrication methods, and printed circuit board (PCB) layout. Prerequisite: EE 2125 or EE 2175 or EE 2145.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for lab EE 2185 A01 - A02. Prerequisites assume familiarity with basic linear circuit analysis techniques. This 2-credit course has 1 lecture and 1 lab per week. Lab work will be in groups; attendance is required out of respect for your lab partner(s). Excused absences must be discussed beforehand.

nodes.title: Electromagnetic Field Theory
nodes.description: Fundamentals of electromagnetic field theory and applications: vector analysis, electric and magnetic fields, potential theory, boundary conditions and boundary value problems, dielectric and magnetic material properties, conductance, capacitance, and inductance, Maxwell-Lorentz theory. Transmission line theory. Prerequisites: PHYS 1550, MATH 2248, and EE 2135.

nodes.title: Teaching Assistantship
nodes.description: Undergraduate student service as a teaching assistant, usually in an introductory-level course in the discipline, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: PLHC Research Experience
nodes.description: Required Junior year course for honors students in CEMS. Explores a range of careers and important research areas in industry, national labs, and academia. Culminates in students identifying a research advisor and a general topic area. Prerequisite: Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Junior standing. Attendance and finding a research advisor and topic

nodes.title: Advanced Circuits Applications
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate standing or instructor permission

nodes.title: Electricity & Magnetism
nodes.description: Fundamental principles of electricity and magnetism; electrostatic fields, and magnetic fields of steady currents. Electric and magnetic properties of matter and electromagnetic energy. Prerequisites: PHYS 1650 or PHYS 1550 and MATH 2248. Credit not given for more than one of PHYS 3300 or EE 3100.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit not given for more than one of PHYS 3300 or EE 3100.

nodes.title: Circuits II
nodes.description: AC circuit analysis and advanced circuit topics with lab component. Topics: AC steady state circuit analysis using phasors, AC power and efficiency, active and passive filters, generalized circuit analysis using the Laplace transform, Fourier series decomposition. Elements of design and sensors. Prerequisite: EE 2125, EE 2175, or EE 2145.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for lab EE 2135 A01-A02

nodes.title: Semiconductor Materials/Devic
nodes.description: Covers Energy band theory, effective mass, band structure and electronic properties of semiconductors. Transport of electrons and holes in bulk materials and across interfaces. MOSFETs, BJTs, pn junctions, and Schottky barriers. Experimental portion of course will cover electronic measurements of semiconductor devices. Credit not awarded for both EE 3440 and EE 5440. Prerequisites: EE 2145, EE 3110, or PHYS 3300. Cross-listed with: PHYS 3675.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for EE 3440 A01 or A02 lab; In person attendance is required. If you miss more than 3 unexcused classes during the semester you will receive an F in the class.

nodes.title: Electrical Engr Concepts
nodes.description: Fundamentals of electrical engineering; DC and AC linear circuit analysis; laboratory component. No credit for more than one of EE 003, EE 2125, EE 2145 and EE 2175. Prerequisites: MATH 1248 or MATH 1242.
nodes.other_requirements: No credit for more than one of EE 003, EE 2125, EE 2145 and EE 2175. Not for BIOM majors; Must register for lab section A01 - A04; No credit for more than one ofEE 2125, EE 2145, orEE 2175; Open to degree and PACE students Please see URL for syllabus from FA 2025. Consider this to be a draft for this coming Fall, as there will be some updates. See above.

nodes.title: Electronics Laboratory
nodes.description: Characteristics and applications of semiconductor devices; inverters and logic characterization; linear amplifiers and applications of operational amplifiers in non-linear circuits. Pre/Co-requisite: EE 3110.
nodes.other_requirements: Junior standing minimum

nodes.title: Electronics I
nodes.description: Physical principles of operation of common semiconductor devices. Analog and digital circuits using diodes and transistors. Electronic circuit analysis and simulation. Prerequisite: PHYS 1550; EE 2135.
nodes.other_requirements: Students should expect to spend 8 to 10 hours outside of class weekly to complete homework, projects and prepare for in-class evaluations.

nodes.title: Electronics Design Project
nodes.description: Design, analyze, simulate, build, test and document electronic circuits that meet engineering specifications. Designs follow standard requirements-based design practices. At least one project will require creating a printed wiring board layout. All projects are documented in formal reports. Focuses on building design and written communication competencies necessary for the engineering profession. Prerequisites: EE 3115, EE 3110; WIL1 course.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for lab EE 3415 A01; WIL1 course Rights and responsibilities in engineering practice and research. Case studies related to engineering ethics. Business culture and its relationship to ethical behavior. Ethics and professional practice as related to professional licensure. Development of individual leadership abilities. Team-based development of written reports and oral presentations. Minimum Junior standing. Students are expected to attend and participate in class discussions which are key to meeting the objectives of the course. Students are expected to complete the assigned readings to ensure informed class discussions. Typical classes will involve discussions in smaller groups of case studies in engineering ethics.

nodes.title: Signals & Systems
nodes.description: Discrete- and continuous-time signals and systems. Input/output descriptions and analysis. Convolution, Fourier analysis, sampling and Laplace transforms. Application to electrical engineering design problems. Prerequisite: MATH 3201. Pre/Co-requisite: EE 2135 recommended.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission recommended. You are expected to know how to program on a computer (in any programming language). In addition, you are expected to know how to solve simple integrals and ordinary differential equations. You must also know complex numbers.

nodes.title: Comp Prog I: for CS majors
nodes.description: Introduction to algorithmic problem solving and computer programming. Designed to provide a foundation for further studies in computer science.

nodes.title: Wearable Sensing
nodes.description: Special topics in developing areas of Electrical Engineering. Prerequisite: Senior standing, or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Senior standing, or Instructor permission.

nodes.title: Power Electronics
nodes.description: An introduction to the field of power conversion using power electronics devices. Topics include Energy and Power, AC-to-DC Converters, DC-to-DC Converters, DC-to-AC Converters, Elements of Control and Design of Power Converters, Applications of Power Electronics in Renewable Energy and Microgrids. Simulations and experiments illustrate concepts. Final project related to renewable energy. Prerequisites: C- or better in EE 2135, EE 2145, EE 2155, or EE 2175.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in EE 2135, EE 2145, EE 2155, or EE 2175. EE 3110

nodes.title: Doctoral Dissertation Research
nodes.description: Research for the Doctoral Dissertation.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate standing only

nodes.title: IS:CyberPowderWirelessNetwork
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: Electronics II
nodes.description: Physical principles of operation of common semiconductor devices. Analog and digital circuits using MOS and bipolar junction transistors. Operational amplifier design. Electronic circuit analysis and simulation. Project-based final. Prerequisite: C- or better in EE 3110.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in EE 3110. Minimum Junior standing. Students are expected to attend and participate in class discussions which are key to meeting the objectives of the course. Students are expected to complete the assigned readings to ensure informed class discussions.

nodes.title: Master's Project Research
nodes.description: Master's Project.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Transitional Calculus
nodes.description: Intended to make the transition from MATH 1212 to MATH 2248. Topics are similar to MATH 1248 but recognizing different backgrounds of students in MATH 1212 versus MATH 1234. Credit will not be given for both MATH 1248 and MATH 1242. No credit following receipt of credit for MATH 2248 or above. Prerequisite: B or better in MATH 1212, or B or better in MATH 1224.
nodes.other_requirements: Open to Degree and PACE students

nodes.title: Integrated Circuit Fabrication
nodes.description: Science and technology of integrated circuit fabrication. Interaction of processing with material properties, electrical performance, economy, and manufacturability. Study of unit processes used to make semiconductor chips. Prerequisite: PHYS 1550 or PHYS 1650. Cross-listed with: PHYS 3165.
nodes.other_requirements: Cross-listed with: PHYS 3165. Semiconductor Certificate Requirement.

nodes.title: System Dynamics
nodes.description: Project-based. Modeling of systems with mechanical, electrical, fluid, and thermal elements. Linear systems analysis. Response of vibratory and feedback systems. Computer simulation. Prerequisite: ME 1120. Co-requisite: MATH 2522 or MATH 2544.
nodes.other_requirements: Co-requisite: MATH 2522 or MATH 2544.

nodes.title: Capstone Design I
nodes.description: Project-based course. Multidisciplinary teams apply their knowledge to design, analyze, build and test a functional prototype that meets client's requirements and solves unique problems. Teams follow engineering design and project management processes such as periodic reports, presentations, meetings, reviews and demonstrations using standard industry tools. Prerequisite: Senior standing in Mechanical or Biomedical Engineering or Instructor permission. Cross-listed with: EE 4100.
nodes.other_requirements: Senior standing in Mechanical or Biomedical Engineering or Instructor permission.

nodes.title: Applied Probability
nodes.description: Foundations of probability, conditioning, and independence. Business, computing, biological, engineering reliability, and quality control applications. Classical discrete and continuous models. Pseudo-random number generation. Prerequisites: MATH 1224 or MATH 1248 or MATH 1242.
nodes.other_requirements: Prereqs enforced by the system:MATH 1224orMATH 1248orMATH 1242;

nodes.title: Control Systems
nodes.description: Analysis and design of control systems; stability, signal flow, performance criteria, classical methods. Analysis of control systems driven by random noise. Laboratory experiments. Prerequisite: C- or better in EE 3150, or C- or better in ME 2120. Pre/Co-requisite: STAT 2430 or STAT 2510. Cross-listed with: ME 3320.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in EE 3150 or C- or better in ME 2120. Pre/Co-requisite: STAT 2430 or STAT 2510. For EE majors & minors only. Must register for lab EE 3515 A01 or A02. Upon permission from instructor, students with A- or higher in Math 3201 may also take this course. You are expected to know how to program on a computer (in any programming language). In addition, you are expected to know: how to solve simple integrals and ordinary differential equations; complex numbers; and Laplace transforms. Attendance is mandatory.

nodes.title: Digital VLSI Circuit Design
nodes.description: Covers the techniques for the design, analysis and layout of digital CMOS circuits and systems. Major topics include MOSFET basics (structure and behavior of a MOSFET, CMOS fabrication, and design rules), detailed analysis of the CMOS circuits and systems (static behavior, ratioed vs. ratioless design), noise margins, computing rise and fall times, delay models, resistance. Prerequisite: Electrical Engineering Graduate student or Instructor permission. Cross-listed with: CMPE 5410.
nodes.other_requirements: Electrical Engineering Graduate student or Instructor permission

nodes.title: Physics for Engineers II
nodes.description: Electricity, magnetism, electromagnetic waves, optics. Without lab. Accompanying optional problem-solving session: PHYS 1560. Prerequisites: PHYS 1500; MATH 1248 or MATH 1242. Co-requisite: MATH 2248.
nodes.other_requirements: CEMS students only

nodes.title: Fundamentals of Physics II
nodes.description: Calculus-based introduction to electricity, magnetism and optics. For students in the natural sciences. Laboratory content is integrated with the lecture. Credit not awarded for both PHYS 1550 and PHYS 1650. Prerequisites: PHYS 1500 or PHYS 1600; credit or concurrent enrollment in MATH 1248.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit not awarded for both PHYS 1550 and PHYS 1650. Credit or concurrent enrollment in MATH 1248.

nodes.title: Adv Engineering Mathematics
nodes.description: Differential equations, Laplace transforms, and systems of differential equations; brief introduction to Fourier series. Examples from engineering and physical sciences. Credit not granted for both MATH 3230 and MATH 3201. No credit for Mathematics majors. Prerequisite: MATH 2248. Co-requisites: Preferred: MATH 2522 or MATH 2544; or MATH 2500.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit not granted for both MATH 3230 and MATH 3201. No credit for Mathematics majors. Co-requisites: Preferred: MATH 2522 or MATH 2544; or MATH 2500. Open to Degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Stochastic Processes
nodes.description: Probability theory, random variables and stochastic processes. Response of linear systems to random inputs. Applications in engineering. Prerequisites: Graduate student in CEMS or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student in CEMS or Instructor permission
